current_fact.fact_text: On 11 May 1812, British Prime Minister Spencer Perceval was assassinated in the lobby of the House of Commons by merchant John Bellingham. He remains the only British prime minister ever killed in office, and his death exposed a tangle of personal grievance, imperial bureaucracy, and the vulnerability of power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

current_fact.match_reason: A shocking political death that forced a nation to pause and reorient fits an episode about sudden rupture, vulnerability, and slowly rebuilding after a life-changing blow.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1813, explorers William Lawson, Gregory Blaxland, and William Wentworth began finding a practical route across Australia’s Blue Mountains. Their path opened the way for European settlers to move beyond the coastal plain, altering the continent’s human geography and impacting Aboriginal lands far inland.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: An overland expedition into unknown terrain pairs with an episode about journeying together over open water—both are about navigation, discovery, and how travel changes the travelers.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 973, Edgar the Peaceful underwent what is considered the first formal coronation ceremony of an English monarch at Bath, even though he had ruled for years already. His wife Ælfthryth was crowned alongside him, making her the first queen of England recorded as crowned in her own right.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A rare early coronation of a queen resonates with the ‘Queen’ imagery and emotional nobility in an episode that centres on love, vulnerability, and powerful feminine energy.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1068, Matilda of Flanders, the politically adept wife of William the Conqueror, was crowned Queen of England in Westminster Abbey. Far from a passive consort, Matilda acted as regent in William’s absence and helped knit together Norman and English elites in the turbulent years after the conquest.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: A medieval queen who quietly wielded power sits well beside an episode celebrating women who seized the stage in rock—both break stereotypes of how women can lead and make noise.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1560, at the Battle of Djerba off the Tunisian coast, the Ottoman navy inflicted a crushing defeat on a combined Spanish-led fleet. The victory marked the high point of Ottoman naval dominance in the Mediterranean, briefly turning the inland sea into almost an Ottoman lake.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A dramatic naval clash that shaped Mediterranean history pairs naturally with a musical voyage around the same sea, especially with Turkish sounds echoing the Ottoman side of the story.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1919, Uruguay became a signatory to the Buenos Aires Convention, a regional treaty on copyright. Long before global digital streaming, it was one step in the slow, often overlooked process of defining how creators’ works would be protected across borders.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: A legal milestone in protecting creative works links well with an episode about a technology that transformed how those works sound—and raised new questions about authenticity and ownership in music.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1987, former Gestapo officer Klaus Barbie, nicknamed the “Butcher of Lyon”, went on trial in Lyon for crimes against humanity committed during World War II. Decades after his torture chambers closed, the proceedings forced France to publicly confront collaboration, resistance, and the memory of the Holocaust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: A sombre, belated reckoning with wartime atrocities suits the moody, reflective textures of trip-hop, a genre that often samples history and shadows to build its atmospheres.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 2011, the Council of Europe’s Istanbul Convention was signed in Turkey’s largest city, aiming to prevent and combat violence against women and domestic violence. The treaty doesn’t just target punishment; it pushes for prevention, education, and support systems that can quietly reshape everyday life.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: A legal framework to combat gender-based violence pairs with an episode amplifying women in hip hop who call out sexism and demand U.N.I.T.Y. in their own fierce language.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1970, the Lubbock tornado’s path across Texas became one of the first urban tornado disasters to be deeply mapped and analysed. Researchers used aerial photography and damage patterns to understand how buildings fail, knowledge that later shaped building codes and safer cities in storm-prone regions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: Researchers turning destruction into knowledge mirrors an episode about the slow, sometimes painful process of learning from difficulty and using it to build something safer and stronger.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 1996, the Everglades crash of ValuJet Flight 592 spurred major reforms in how hazardous materials are declared, stored, and regulated on aircraft. The tragedy turned obscure cargo-hold procedures into matters of public concern, showing that safety can hinge on unseen details.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

queue.match_reason: An accident that led to stricter discipline around hidden risks connects metaphorically with an episode about physical discipline, training, and the often unseen work required to move safely and powerfully.

queue.fact_text: On 11 May 2011, the Council of Europe’s Istanbul Convention opened for signature in a city that bridges continents. By framing domestic violence as a societal and structural issue rather than a private misfortune, it encouraged countries to rethink justice, support systems, and cultural narratives around harm at home.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: A convention challenging deep-rooted patterns of violence sits alongside flamenco, a tradition that has long given voice to suffering, resilience, and emotional truth in intimate spaces.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2009, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched STS-125, the final servicing mission to the Hubble Space Telescope. Astronauts essentially “operated on” a 19-year-old observatory in orbit, replacing gyroscopes, adding new instruments, and giving Hubble a new lease on life that still shapes our view of the cosmos.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic engineering feat that turned 'empty space' into a canvas for discovery pairs with an episode about reframing emptiness as creative space and shifting perspective.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2024, a series of intense geomagnetic storms began, producing some of the most powerful auroras seen since the 2003 Halloween storms. The solar outbursts were so strong that northern lights shimmered over skies far closer to Earth’s equator than usual, briefly turning our planet into a global light show.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

published.match_reason: The storm-driven auroras and space weather feel like a real-life 'cosmic disco', matching the cosmic dancefloor vibe and electronic shimmer of this episode.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2024, the Eurovision Song Contest in Malmö crowned Switzerland’s Nemo as winner with “The Code”. Their victory quietly rewrote the contest’s history, making Nemo Eurovision’s first non-binary champion and turning a pop performance into a milestone for gender diversity on one of Earth’s most-watched stages.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: Eurovision is a giant shared dancefloor where music and identity collide; an episode about claiming space on the dancefloor echoes Nemo’s boundary-breaking presence on that stage.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1997, IBM’s supercomputer Deep Blue defeated world chess champion Garry Kasparov in their final game, winning the match. It was the first time a computer beat a reigning world champion in a classical chess match, symbolising a turning point where silicon intuition began rivaling human strategy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A landmark in human–machine relations pairs well with an episode about our tangled dependence on technology, especially with a track called "Computer Love" in the mix.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1998, India conducted three underground nuclear tests at Pokhran, surprising much of the world. The tests, code-named Operation Shakti, asserted India’s nuclear capabilities and triggered a wave of global debate on security, deterrence, and the balance of power in South Asia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A controversial display of nuclear power and geopolitical tension fits the raw, politically charged energy and anti-establishment voice of a Rage Against the Machine–focused episode.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1996, ValuJet Flight 592 crashed into the Florida Everglades after a fire broke out in the cargo hold, killing all 110 people on board. The blaze was traced to improperly stored chemical oxygen generators, forcing the aviation world to rethink how ‘invisible’ safety systems and regulations are handled.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A tragedy born partly from overlooked details and systemic habits pairs with an episode about paying closer, more intentional attention to the systems and choices we usually leave on autopilot.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1970, a devastating F5 tornado tore through Lubbock, Texas, killing 26 people and causing massive damage. The storm’s path became a case study that helped scientists better understand urban tornado dynamics and improve warning systems that save lives today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A violent storm that reshaped weather science connects with an episode built around rain, resilience, and finding hope after the clouds pass.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2011, a magnitude 5.1 earthquake struck the town of Lorca in southeastern Spain, killing nine people and damaging historic buildings. Unusually for such a moderate quake, much of the destruction came from how local geology amplified the shaking, turning the town into a natural laboratory for seismic risk in urban areas.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A sudden reminder of Earth’s tectonic power pairs with an episode that lingers on the planet’s other extremes—deep oceans and polar seas—inviting awe for the forces shaping our world.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2022, the Burmese military carried out the Mon Taing Pin massacre in Sagaing Region, executing at least 37 villagers. The atrocity highlighted how remote rural communities can become invisible frontlines, where everyday life collides with brutal state violence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A brutal act of state violence connects with an episode centred on a fiercely political artist whose work confronts oppression and gives voice to communities under pressure.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2014, tear gas fired by police into soccer stands in Kinshasa triggered a deadly stampede at Stade Tata Raphaël, killing 15 people and injuring dozens. A match meant to be a joyful spectacle became a lesson in how crowd dynamics, fear, and heavy-handed policing can turn celebration into catastrophe within minutes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #23 - Morning Dubs
episode.description: Recorded while still processing Houghton Festival. One of the mornings there, waking up in the woods to a reggae DJ set — no phone signal, just trees, music, and loved ones. A slow selection of reggae, dub, and morning rhythms. Dubs instead of drops. Start your day slow — with space to breathe.
songs: The Gaylads - Joy in the Morning
King Tubby - King Tubby Meets the Rockers Uptown

published.match_reason: A tragedy at a sporting event underscores how fragile public gatherings can be; the grounding, reflective dub of this episode offers a gentler vision of crowds, sound, and shared space.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 2011, the Istanbul Convention was opened for signature in its namesake city, becoming the first legally binding instrument in Europe specifically targeting violence against women and domestic violence. It reframed such abuse as a human rights violation, not a private matter, pushing countries to change law, policing, and social attitudes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: A landmark treaty to protect women aligns with an episode honouring powerful female voices whose music embodies resilience, self-worth, and resistance to abuse.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1889, bandits ambushed U.S. Army paymaster Joseph Wham in Arizona Territory, stealing over $28,000 in gold and silver. Despite being outgunned and wounded, several Buffalo Soldiers fought to protect the payroll, and two later received the Medal of Honor—while the robbers were sensationally acquitted in court.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

published.match_reason: A little-known story of courage and injustice in the 19th-century American West pairs with an episode about legacies that endure—echoed in classic, soulful tracks that honour struggle and bravery.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1894, around 4,000 workers at the Pullman Palace Car Company launched a wildcat strike over wage cuts and high rents in the company town. The protest escalated into a nationwide railway boycott and one of the most significant labour conflicts in U.S. history, reshaping debates about workers’ rights and federal intervention.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: Workers refusing to be boxed into exploitative conditions connects with an episode about breaking out of boxes and asserting identity, powered by politically conscious hip hop.

published.fact_text: On 11 May 1812, British Prime Minister Spencer Perceval was assassinated in the lobby of the House of Commons by merchant John Bellingham. He remains the only British prime minister ever killed in office, and his death exposed a tangle of personal grievance, imperial bureaucracy, and the vulnerability of power.
